idioms.definition: To make more effort than is expected or required; to do more than is necessary
examples: She always goes the extra mile for her clients.
If you want to stand out, you need to go the extra mile.
The team went the extra mile to finish the project before the deadline.
idioms.usageTip: Used to describe exceptional effort or dedication. Common in professional and customer service contexts.

idioms.definition: To do your fair share of the work; to contribute as much effort as others in a group
examples: Everyone needs to pull their weight if we want to finish on time.
The manager felt that some team members weren't pulling their weight.
She's great – she always pulls her weight and then some.
idioms.usageTip: Often used when someone is not contributing enough to a shared task. Frequently heard in the negative: 'not pulling their weight'.

idioms.definition: To endure a painful or difficult situation with courage and without complaining
examples: I hate going to the dentist, but I'll just have to bite the bullet.
He bit the bullet and told his boss that the project would be late.
Sometimes you just have to bite the bullet and make a tough decision.
idioms.usageTip: Often used when someone faces something unpleasant but unavoidable. The origin comes from soldiers biting a bullet during surgery before anaesthesia.

idioms.definition: To work or study until very late at night
examples: She was burning the midnight oil to finish her thesis.
The whole team burned the midnight oil before the product launch.
He's been burning the midnight oil all week preparing for the presentation.
idioms.usageTip: Usually implies hard work and dedication. The image comes from working by candlelight or an oil lamp late at night.

idioms.definition: Whatever happens; regardless of the circumstances; reliably and without fail
examples: He delivers the newspapers every morning, come rain or shine.
She shows up to every training session, come rain or shine.
Come rain or shine, I'll be at the finish line cheering for you.
idioms.usageTip: Emphasises reliability and commitment. Often used to describe someone's dedication to a routine or promise.

idioms.definition: To make a firm promise; to solemnly commit to doing something
examples: I give you my word that I'll get it done by Friday.
He gave her his word that he would never lie to her again.
You gave me your word – I'm counting on you.
idioms.usageTip: More formal and serious than a casual promise. Saying 'I give you my word' implies strong personal honour and commitment.

fillInExercises.sentence: She always ___ for her students, staying late to give extra help.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go the extra mile' means to make more effort than is required.

fillInExercises.sentence: He has a lot of talent but the rest of the team feel he's not really ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pull your weight' means to contribute your fair share to a group effort.

fillInExercises.sentence: The deadline was tomorrow, so the whole team was ___ to finish the report.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work or study very late into the night.

fillInExercises.sentence: I really don't want to have this conversation, but I'll just have to ___ and get it over with.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bite the bullet' means to endure something unpleasant with courage.

fillInExercises.sentence: He trains every morning ___ – even in the middle of winter.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come rain or shine' means regardless of circumstances, reliably and without fail.

fillInExercises.sentence: I ___ that the money will be returned to you by the end of the month.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Give your word' means to make a firm, serious promise.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: The client wants the full report by Thursday morning. We have two days.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.text: Then we're going to have to [burn the midnight oil|burn the midnight oil] tonight and tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Agreed. And everyone needs to [pull their weight|pull their weight] – no disappearing at five o'clock.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.text: I'll [give you my word|give you my word] – I'm not going anywhere until this is done.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: What about Marcus? He's been a bit slow this week.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.text: I'll talk to him. Sometimes you just have to [bite the bullet|bite the bullet] and have the difficult conversation.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Good idea. And if we can [go the extra mile|go the extra mile] with the data visualisation, the client will be really impressed.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.text: Let's do it. I'll order some food – we're going to be here a while.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: I wanted to say – your performance this quarter has been outstanding.

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: Thank you. I really tried to [go the extra mile|go the extra mile] with the new client accounts.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: It shows. You're always here when we need you, [come rain or shine|come rain or shine].

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: I believe in the work we're doing. I [give you my word|give you my word] I'll keep the same level of commitment.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: And the rest of the team notice how hard you work. You always [pull your weight|pull your weight].

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: I had to [bite the bullet|bite the bullet] a few times and work weekends, but it was worth it.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: do your fair share of the work
work or study very late at night

quizExercises.sentence: She submitted every assignment on time ___, even when she was ill.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Come rain or shine' means regardless of circumstances, reliably.

quizExercises.sentence: We had to ___ before the final exam – the library was full of students at midnight.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work or study very late into the night.

quizExercises.question: Which situation describes 'biting the bullet'?
options: Doing something fun and easy without much thought
Facing a difficult or unpleasant task with courage instead of avoiding it
Making extra effort to impress someone
quizExercises.explanation: 'Bite the bullet' means accepting and facing something painful or difficult without complaining.

quizExercises.question: If someone is NOT pulling their weight in a group project, what are they doing?
options: Contributing less effort than the rest of the team
quizExercises.explanation: 'Pull your weight' means to contribute your fair share. Not pulling your weight means contributing less than expected.
